title: Nonlinear descent on moduli of local systems
speaker: Junho Peter Whang
abstract: In 1880, Markoff studied a cubic Diophantine equation in three variables now known as the Markoff equation, and observed that its integral solutions satisfy a form of nonlinear descent. Generalizing this, we consider families of log Calabi-Yau varieties arising as moduli spaces for local systems on topological surfaces, and prove a structure theorem for their integral points using mapping class group dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Critical Diophantine framework and log Calabi-Yau condition
proof_stages.summary: A naive count for integral points on polynomial level sets singles out the critical case degree equals ambient dimension, which is reformulated via compactification as the vanishing of the log canonical divisor.

proof_stages.label: Markoff nonlinear descent prototype and class-number analogy
proof_stages.summary: The Markoff cubic surface is presented as the guiding model where Vieta involutions and coordinate symmetries give a nonlinear group action with finitely many integral orbits, analogous to Borel-Harish-Chandra finiteness for linear arithmetic group actions.

proof_stages.label: SL2 character varieties with fixed boundary traces
proof_stages.summary: For a compact oriented surface with boundary, the talk defines the fixed-boundary-trace SL2-character variety X_k, its trace-coordinate functions, its integral model, and the theorem that these varieties are log Calabi-Yau and specialize to the Markoff surface in the once-punctured torus case.

proof_stages.label: Main mapping-class-group descent theorem
proof_stages.summary: The mapping class group is made to act on X_k and its integral points, the nondegenerate locus is defined by excluding trace ±2 along every essential curve, and the main theorem asserts finiteness of nondegenerate integral orbits plus a finite parabolic description of the degenerate locus.

proof_stages.label: Integrable-system reduction via pants decompositions and Dehn twists
proof_stages.summary: Dehn twists along the curves of a pants decomposition give commuting transformations preserving trace-map fibers that are algebraic tori, reducing descent to finding uniformly bounded pants-decomposition traces and then moving along torus fibers into a compact set.

proof_stages.label: Uniform bounded-curve theorem as a systole bound
proof_stages.summary: The bounded trace problem is converted into a bounded translation-length problem for SL2 acting on hyperbolic three-space, leading to the systole of a local system and the key theorem that this systole is uniformly bounded on X_k in terms of the boundary traces and topology.

proof_stages.label: Harmonic-section and systolic-inequality proof
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the classical hyperbolic-surface systole bound, the proof constructs a twisted H^3-bundle from a local system, pulls back the hyperbolic metric by a harmonic section, controls volume and boundary lengths, and applies Gromov-type systolic inequalities to obtain the uniform systole bound and hence complete the descent argument.

examples.name: Multiplicative group and algebraic tori
examples.note: The speaker uses G_m, with logarithmic form dz/z having simple poles at 0 and infinity, and its products to make the log Calabi-Yau condition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker checks the multiplicative group is log Calabi–Yau by embedding it in [35mP[0m1 and identifying the form dz/z with simple poles at 0 and infinity, then extends this to tori.

examples.name: Sum of three cubes surface
examples.note: The affine cubic x^3+y^3+z^3=k is cited to show how little is known even for simple log Calabi-Yau Diophantine problems such as deciding integral solvability for specific k.
examples.engagement_reason: The sum-of-three-cubes surface is named with its equation and the open case k=33, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it.

examples.name: Hyperbolic surface systole and disk-area argument
examples.note: The Fuchsian/uniformization case, especially the genus-two embedded-disk area bound, visibly motivates the later strategy of proving a uniform local-system systole bound by controlling volume and boundary length of an auxiliary metric.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the systole argument by relating the systole to an embedded disk radius, comparing disk area to total hyperbolic area, and invoking Gauss–Bonnet.

passes.reason: The systole is by definition a shortest essential curve and the disk-area argument proves a bound rather than opposing a claim.
